era_context: The Iron Age II Levant and Eastern Mediterranean. Ashurnasirpal II is in the final years of his reign (he will die c. 859 BCE). Assyrian power is at its zenith. In the Levant, the Kingdom of Israel under the Omride dynasty (possibly Ahab, r. c. 874–853 BCE) is a significant regional power. Aram-Damascus under Ben-Hadad II is a rival. Phoenician city-states are prosperous. Egypt is fragmented under the late 21st Dynasty. Greece is in the early Archaic period.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Specific year-by-year events in most regions

events.title: Possible reign of Ahab of Israel (traditional dating c. 874–853 BCE)
events.description: The Kingdom of Israel under the Omride dynasty is a major regional power in the Levant during this period. Ahab, if he is reigning in 875 BCE, is the son of Omri and a significant figure in Levantine politics. He is known from both biblical texts and Assyrian records (the Tel Dan Stele and Shalmaneser III's annals mention him as a military opponent). His reign is marked by military conflicts with Aram-Damascus and later with Assyria. The exact dating of Ahab's reign remains debated among scholars, with traditional dates placing him c. 874–853 BCE.
key_figures: Ahab (King of Israel, traditional dating c. 874–853 BCE)

sources.name: Tel Dan Stele (Aramaic inscription mentioning 'House of David')
Shalmaneser III's annals (mentioning Ahab as 'Ahabbu of Israel')

events.certainty_note: The dating of Ahab's reign is debated. Traditional biblical chronology places him c. 874–853 BCE, but some scholars argue for a lower chronology (c. 869–848 BCE). The year 875 BCE is assigned here by convention within the traditional chronological framework, but the actual year of Ahab's reign is uncertain by ±5–10 years.
cross_references: Relates to conflicts with Aram-Damascus and later Assyrian campaigns under Shalmaneser III (c. 853 BCE)

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Ahab's reign is one of the most contested issues in Iron Age Levantine chronology. Traditional biblical chronology places him c. 874–853 BCE, but the 'low chronology' advocated by some scholars (e.g., Israel Finkelstein) places him c. 869–848 BCE or even later. The Tel Dan Stele, which mentions the 'House of David,' is dated to the 9th century BCE but does not provide precise dating for Ahab. Shalmaneser III's annals mention Ahab at the Battle of Qarqar (traditionally dated 853 BCE), but the exact year is debated. No contemporary Israelite sources from 875 BCE survive. The biblical account of Ahab is compiled centuries after his reign and reflects later theological and political concerns. Modern scholarship increasingly questions whether the biblical portrait of Ahab is historically reliable.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 875 BCE is heavily dependent on later biblical compilation, Assyrian royal inscriptions (which are not contemporary with 875 BCE but refer to events decades later), and archaeological evidence from Iron Age Levantine sites. The dating of Ahab's reign is one of the most debated topics in Iron Age chronology, with scholars divided between 'high' and 'low' chronologies that differ by 10–20 years. No contemporary Israelite written sources survive from this period. The surviving record is biased toward Assyrian and later biblical perspectives. Egyptian and Greek sources do not provide information about the Levant in 875 BCE.

graph_edges.note: Ahab's reign culminates in the Battle of Qarqar (traditionally dated 853 BCE) against Shalmaneser III of Assyria
